title: Dissipative Channels Determine Open Electromagnetic Quantization
authors: Hyunwoo Choi, Junwoo Gim, Thomas E. Roth, Weng Cho Chew, Dong-Yeop Na
abstract: We formulate a quantization scheme for open electromagnetic systems with arbitrary passive boundary conditions. Rather than specifying reservoirs phenomenologically, the method identifies them from the dissipation geometry of the Maxwell operator. Factoring the imaginary part of the Maxwell operator gives a bosonic realization of the field operator and separates the fluctuation channels into medium-assisted reservoirs from material absorption and boundary-assisted reservoirs from exchange through the open boundary. Depending on the boundary condition, the latter become free-space radiation modes, impedance-load channels, guided port modes, or more general boundary channels. Green-function input-output relations then follow as an application, yielding frequency-dependent scattering and noise kernels without Markov or single-mode assumptions. To illustrate the practical application, we consider a lossy structure with mixed impedance and outgoing boundaries, and photonic integrated circuit configurations with waveguide port boundaries.
summary: The paper introduces a novel quantization scheme for open electromagnetic systems by deriving environmental reservoirs from the Maxwell operator's dissipation geometry. It successfully formulates input-output relations that accurately describe scattering and noise kernels in complex structures like PICs, correctly capturing non-Markovian spectral effects.
main_problem: To formulate a rigorous quantization scheme for open electromagnetic systems by deriving the necessary environmental reservoirs directly from the dissipation geometry of the Maxwell operator, rather than assuming them phenomenologically.
main_result: The method successfully derives frequency-dependent scattering and noise kernels ($S(\omega), N(\omega)$) that are consistent with fundamental quantum electrodynamics principles, unifying material absorption and open boundary leakage into a single framework.
method: It employs Gram factorization on the imaginary part of the Maxwell operator to decompose dissipation into medium-assisted (material loss) and boundary-assisted (radiation/port coupling) channels, leading to an input-output relation for bosonic field operators.
model_or_system: The system is open electromagnetic fields governed by the frequency-domain Maxwell equation. It models structures like photonic integrated circuits (PICs) or lossy slabs with arbitrary passive boundary conditions (e.g., impedance loads, waveguide ports).
key_observables: Frequency-dependent scattering kernels $S(\omega)$, noise kernels $N(\omega)$, Purcell factors, and Hong–Ou–Mandel (HOM) interference correlation functions.
important_parameters: Material permittivity ($\epsilon''$), boundary conditions (impedance/port), frequency $\omega$, and the structure's geometry (2D PIC layouts).
main_assumptions: The system is passive, ensuring the dissipation operator $D(\omega)$ is positive semi-definite; it assumes thermal equilibrium for standard fluctuation-dissipation theorem applications.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate comparisons of Purcell factors and HOM interference kernels between full (non-Markovian) and simplified (Markovian) models in PICs, demonstrating spectral dependence effects.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by defining the dissipation operator $D(\omega)$, factorizing it into boundary/material parts, deriving the input-output relation $\hat{a}_{	ext{out}}(\omega) = S(\omega)\hat{a}_{	ext{in}}(\omega) + N(\omega)\hat{f}_{	ext{int}}(\omega)$, and applying this to calculate measurable quantities like scattering matrices and correlation functions.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, first-principles method for quantizing open quantum systems in electromagnetism, directly linking macroscopic material losses and boundary coupling to the fundamental bosonic structure of the field operators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: One-dimensional waveguide with an impedance and outgoing BA channel, with a lossy slab providing MA channels. (a) Structure schematic. (b) Purcell factor for an emitter inside the slab (x0 = 5.5 µm). (c) Purcell factor for an emitter outside the slab (x0 = 8.5 µm).

FIG. 3: HOM interference for non-Markovian splitter responses. (a) HOM interference kernel K(ω) for the broadband and ring-loaded splitters, with the photon spectrum shown in orange. (b) HOM correlation for the full S(ω) response and the Markovian flat-S(ωc) reference.

FIG. 2: Designed photonic integrated circuit configurations and port-channel modes. (a) Broadband 50/50 splitter. (b) Ring-loaded interferometric splitter. (c) Dispersion of the local slab-port cross section, showing the two guided modes retained in the calculation. (d) Transverse profiles of the two guided port modes at λ = 1550 nm. The shaded region denotes the waveguide core.

Figure 3(a,b) shows the resulting finite-bandwidth response. The broadband coupler has an almost flat K(ω) over the photon spectrum, so all spectral com- ponents undergo nearly the same transformation and the full calculation agrees with the flat-S(ωc) refer- ence. In contrast, the ring-loaded splitter is tuned near K(ωc) ≃0, but the ring resonance makes K(ω) vary strongly across the photon bandwidth. This produces two finite-bandwidth non-Markovian sig- natures. The dip is shallower because different spec- tral components no longer satisfy the same can- cellation condition, leading to incomplete destruc- tive interference after integration over the photon spectrum.
